# Saving the Appearances

**Record ID:** wiki:ancient-astronomy-eo-saving-the-appearances

***Sozein ta phainomena*** — *saving the appearances* — is the demand, originating in ancient Greek astronomical practice, that a theory must be *faithful to the phenomena*: it must contain every observation without discarding, overriding, or distorting any of them. The term does not mean "explain the appearances" or "prove the appearances." It means: *do not lose them*.

EO adopts this as its first and prior standard. Before EO can be evaluated as an account of what transformation *truly is*, it must meet the Ptolemaic standard: the nine operators and the 27-cell substrate must be able to receive every described transformation in every domain — without remainder, without forcing the description into a shape it does not have, and without requiring that some appearances be set aside until later.

## 1. The Standard

The phrase entered astronomical discourse as a description of what mathematical models must do. A model that predicts some motions but requires you to ignore others has not saved the appearances — it has selected them. The demand is for completeness, not correctness: a system that preserves all the observations intact, even before it can say why they are that way.

Ptolemy's *Almagest* is the greatest worked example. Its mechanism: deferents and epicycles, equants and eccentrics, a layered geometry of circular motions composable into any observed planetary path. The Ptolemaic system was not ontologically correct. The planets do not actually move on circles. But it could *contain* every observed planetary position with extraordinary precision, and it did so for over fourteen centuries.

What the Ptolemaic system demonstrated:

Adequacy of the grammar precedes truth of the ontology. You do not need to know why Mars traces the path it traces. You need a notation sufficient to describe the path. Only once the description is adequate can the question of causes be meaningfully posed.

Completeness is the harder and earlier demand. A system that explains five planets correctly and cannot account for a sixth has failed the prior test, regardless of the beauty of its causal account for the five.

The standard is falsifiable. An appearance that cannot be assigned an address in the notation — that falls outside what the grammar can say — is an empirical challenge to the system's adequacy, not a philosophical objection to its ontology.

Thomas Aquinas described the epistemic status precisely: a theory like the Ptolemaic system is established because thereby the sensible appearances can be explained — not, however, as if this proof were sufficient, forasmuch as some other theory might explain them. Adequacy is not uniqueness. Saving the appearances does not close the question of what the appearances mean.

## 2. Where We Stand: The Pre-Ptolemaic State of Change Description

For planetary astronomy, Ptolemy had appearances to save. Centuries of Babylonian and Greek observation had regularised the celestial motions into a corpus: positions, periods, retrograde arcs, occultations.

The Babylonians were not cataloguing stars as things. They were cataloguing relational positions over time: angular separations, conjunction periods, retrograde arcs, how each body moved relative to every other body as the record accumulated across generations. The appearances they preserved were fundamentally relational and temporal — not objects but patterns of relation between objects as they changed.

Ptolemy inherited a corpus of how things stood in transformation relative to each other. The grammar he built was a notation for saving that relational-temporal record, not for describing what planets are.

The equivalent for EO is a corpus of relational dependencies between transformation types: what precedes what, what depends on what, what produces what, what cannot occur without what. The helix dependency ordering is the proposed theory. The corpus that would test it is a systematic cross-domain record of those dependencies.

EO has partial versions: the cross-linguistic verb distribution (27 languages), the cross-cultural crosswalk (40+ traditions), the relational algebra mapping. None is yet the full observational record. The tablets are incomplete.

What exists instead is a collection of isolated, domain-specific notations, each adequate within its own domain and mutually unintelligible across domains: differential equations (physics), state machines (computation), aspect systems (linguistics), process ontologies (philosophy), developmental stage models (psychology). A phase transition, a perceptual pop-out, a grammatical aspect shift, a species-level evolutionary event, a conceptual revolution — all are transformations, but the disciplines that study them have no shared grammar for what kind of transformation each is.

This is the pre-Ptolemaic condition. The grammar for saving the appearances of transformation does not yet exist as a shared, cross-domain vocabulary. That grammar is what EO proposes to be.

## 3. Ontological Bias and the Priority of the Given-Log

The Ptolemaic standard says: save the appearances before theorizing about what produces them. This demands more than methodological humility. It demands that we notice when our theories are quietly discarding appearances before we even begin.

### The entity bias

Western metaphysics since Aristotle has organized itself around a particular kind of subject: bounded, self-identical, persisting through time, with an interior that processes and an exterior that is processed. Animals are the prototype. The relational model inherits this — a database row is a bounded entity with properties that persist until overwritten. BFO's ontology of continuants and occurrents presupposes the same shape: things that endure and things that happen to them.

This ontological commitment is not a finding. It is a frame. And the frame has consequences for what counts as an appearance worth saving.

### The mycelial test case

Consider a fungal mycelium exhibiting negative autotropism — turning away from itself. This requires a maintained operational distinction between self and non-self: the hypha encounters another hypha, classifies it, and acts differently depending on the classification. Self: turn away. Non-self, same species: possibly fuse (anastomosis) or compete. Non-self, different species: different response again.

The entity-biased observer faces a choice. Either the fungus is "doing something like cognition" (which feels like overreach, because it has no nervous system) or it is "just chemistry" (which feels like underreach, because chemistry alone does not explain why the system maintains a revisable self-boundary, evaluates relationship health under stress, and reorganizes its own topology based on results).

Both responses discard appearances to protect a frame. The first imports a nervous-system prototype and asks whether the fungus measures up. The second strips the phenomenon of its relational complexity because the complexity does not fit the entity model. Neither saves the appearances.

### What the Given-Log requires

EO's Given-Log architecture is designed to resist exactly this. The log records what was observed, not what the observation means. Agent, mode of givenness, context envelope — these are the epistemic constitution of every entry. The log does not ask whether the fungus is "really" conscious. It asks: what capacities are observably present?

The observations, stated without ontological commitment:

The mycelium directs resources differentially based on environmental gradients — SIG (attention directed). It maintains a distinction between self and non-self that it acts on — DEF (definition of what holds, actively maintained). It draws boundaries: negative autotropism, territory establishment — SEG (boundary drawn). It connects across boundaries: anastomosis, mycorrhizal exchange — CON (relationship established). It produces emergent wholes irreducible to components: the network itself — SYN (synthesis). It evaluates against criteria that contradict immediate gradient logic: when experimentally stressed trees give less carbon, the fungus increases colonization by 110% and hyphal length by 340%, doubling down on a failing host — EVA (judgment rendered against a standard that is not the immediate gradient). It reorganizes its own topology based on results: pruning dead-end hyphae, reinforcing productive paths, operating across multiple holonic layers from hypha to network to mycorrhizal association to ecosystem — REC (frame restructuring).

Seven of nine operators directly observed. By cumulative capacity — each operator in the helix contains all those below it — demonstrating REC at position 9 establishes the full set. Nine capacities. Full helix. In an organism with no nervous system, no central processing, and no bounded self.

The Given-Log does not record "the fungus is conscious." It records "these capacities are observably present in this system, as observed by this agent, in this mode, within this frame." The interpretation — what it means that a decentralized network with no brain instantiates the full operator set — belongs to the Meant-Graph. And the Meant-Graph is where theories are tested, revised, and sometimes abandoned.

### The deeper methodological point

We cannot fully untangle our biases from what we are able to see. Our ontological commitments shape which appearances we notice, which we record, which we explain away, and which we never look for. The entity bias is not the only bias — it is merely the most pervasive and the best-documented.

The defense is not to claim bias-freedom. It is to start from the appearances, stated as carefully as we know how, with provenance attached, and build upward. The Given-Log's epistemic architecture — agent, mode, context — makes the observation's frame visible as part of the record. The biases do not disappear. They become legible. Patterns in what gets recorded and what gets explained away are themselves appearances — and they too must be saved.

However the world actually works — however distant the real structure is from our models of it — the models must at minimum save the appearances. EO does not claim its nine operators map the real structure of transformation as it exists independent of observation. It claims they save the appearances: they can receive every described transformation in every domain without remainder, without forcing the description into a shape it does not have, and without requiring that some appearances be set aside because the observer's ontological commitments say they should not be there.

Truth in EO is not a value the Given-Log holds. It is a limit the system approaches. As observations accumulate from multiple positions and frames are refined through DEF and REC, the Meant-Graph converges asymptotically toward what those observations are about. The Ptolemaic standard is what gets you moving toward it.

## 4. The Ptolemaic Claim EO Must Establish

EO's first and prior claim — prior to any ontological claim about what transformation *is* — is:

The nine operators and the 27-cell substrate constitute a grammar sufficient to assign a structural address to any described transformation in any domain, without remainder and without distortion.

This is the Ptolemaic claim. It is not the claim that EO's operators describe the actual causal structure of transformation. It is not the claim that the triadic axes track real ontological strata. It is not the claim that the helix dependency ordering is discovered rather than constructed. Those are the Newtonian questions — they come after, and they require the Ptolemaic foundation to be solid first.

The Ptolemaic claim is strictly about expressive completeness. Can every transformation anyone points to receive an address? Can every appearance be saved without loss?

**What the claim requires:**

A description of a transformation — in any domain, in any tradition, in any notation — must be expressible using the nine operators as a vocabulary, producing an address in the 27-cell substrate. The address must:

1. Assign the transformation to one or more operator positions without forcing it into a shape it does not have.
2. Not require the description to be trimmed, simplified, or corrected before it can be addressed.
3. Not leave a remainder — a component of the description that has no address in the grammar.

**What the claim does not require:**

The Ptolemaic claim does not require that EO's address is the only correct address, or that different analysts would assign the same address, or that the address reveals anything about why the transformation has the structure it does. Ptolemy's epicycle calculation for Mars was not the only way to describe Mars's path. It was a way that worked. That is all the standard demands at this stage.

## 5. The Residue Test

The correct adversarial stress test for EO at this stage is not "is EO's ontology true?" It is: does EO have the expressive completeness to contain the appearances?

The test procedure:

1. Take transformation descriptions from domains EO was not designed for: immunological response, jazz improvisation, tectonic subduction, grief, ecological succession, the development of a legal norm, the emergence of a new literary genre.
2. Attempt to assign each a structural address using the nine operators and the substrate.
3. Identify the residue: anything in the description that cannot be assigned an address without distortion.

Residue is the empirical challenge. If a class of transformations consistently produces remainder — something in the description that falls outside what the grammar can say — that is a genuine falsification of the Ptolemaic claim, and it identifies the direction in which the grammar needs to be extended or revised.

Two outcomes are not residue and should not be treated as such:

**Underdetermination**: multiple valid addresses for the same description. The Ptolemaic system offered multiple epicycle configurations for the same planetary path. Underdetermination means the grammar has more expressive power than any single description requires — which is not a failure.

**Empty cells**: the grammar contains addresses to which no known transformation has been assigned. The empty cell at SYN x Ground — the position that has returned zero verbs across 32,000+ verbs in 27 languages and zero transformations across every domain tested — is not a gap in the coverage of appearances. It is itself an appearance: the structural observation that a particular kind of transformation is either impossible or radically impoverished in human practice. Saving this appearance means preserving the void as data, not filling it in.

## 6. The Ptolemaic Claim and the Newtonian Claim

The history of astronomy distinguishes two moments:

**The Ptolemaic moment**: a grammar adequate to the appearances. Predictively powerful, faithful to observation, expressively complete — but silent on causes. The Almagest does not explain why planets move. It describes how they appear to move with sufficient precision that the description can be trusted for navigation, calendrics, and prediction.

**The Newtonian moment**: a causal account that explains why the appearances are what they are. Newton's law of universal gravitation does not merely predict planetary positions — it explains why planets follow the paths they do, derives Kepler's empirical laws from first principles, and unifies celestial and terrestrial mechanics under a single causal framework. The Ptolemaic grammar becomes a special case of the Newtonian account.

For EO, these map onto distinct claims at different stages of development:

| Stage Claim Falsification |
| --- |
| Ptolemaic | The nine operators constitute a grammar sufficient to address any described transformation | A transformation description that produces genuine remainder in the grammar |
| Newtonian | The nine operators and their dependency ordering reflect the actual causal structure of transformation | A case where the dependency ordering is shown to be contingent or domain-specific rather than structural |

EO currently has strong evidence bearing on the Ptolemaic claim — the cross-linguistic verb corpus, the cross-cultural crosswalk, the relational algebra mapping, the developmental biology correspondence — but this evidence is not yet consolidated into a systematic residue test. The Newtonian claim has not been formally attempted and may not be the right frame: whether transformation has "causes" in the Newtonian sense is itself an open question.

The development priority is clear: the Ptolemaic claim must be established — or its failures identified — before the Newtonian claim can be meaningfully posed.

## 7. Why This Standard Is Prior to Proof

The point is not that EO is modest. The point is that the demand for adequacy-of-appearance precedes the demand for truth-of-explanation in any rigorous scientific programme.

Asking whether EO is "true" before it has demonstrated expressive completeness is asking the wrong question at the wrong stage. It is asking Newton's question before Ptolemy's work is done. The question "are the nine operators the real structure of change?" cannot be evaluated until we know that the nine operators can say everything that needs to be said about how change appears. If the grammar is incomplete — if there are genuine appearances it cannot contain — then the ontological question is moot: the grammar is inadequate before it is true or false.

Conversely: a grammar that saves all the appearances has earned the right to be taken seriously as an ontological claim, even if it has not yet demonstrated that its structural account is causally correct. Ptolemy earned fourteen centuries of authority not by proving heliocentrism wrong but by producing a system adequate to the phenomena. That adequacy was real and consequential work. It had to be done before Copernicus, Kepler, and Newton could do theirs.

EO's situation is precisely this. The current fragmentation of change-description across incompatible domain notations is not a theoretical problem — it is a pre-theoretical one. The grammar for saving the appearances of transformation does not yet exist as a shared, cross-domain vocabulary. That grammar is what EO proposes to be. Whether the proposal succeeds is a testable question. But the test is adequacy first, truth second.

## 8. Relation to Other EO Articles

The Ptolemaic standard connects to several structural features of EO discussed elsewhere:

**Bivalent Compression and Dimensional Poverty**: the fragmentation of change-description across incompatible notations is a dimensional-compression problem — each notation operates on one or two faces of the substrate without access to the full three-dimensional structure. A shared grammar for transformation appearances requires the full 27-cell space.

**The Empty Cell (SYN x Ground)**: the most important single piece of evidence bearing on EO's Ptolemaic claim. Its persistent absence across all tested domains is itself an appearance — one that a complete grammar of transformation must be able to contain and name, not explain away.

**Cross-Linguistic Findings**: the 32,000-verb corpus is the largest systematic attempt to test whether the nine operators constitute a universal vocabulary for transformation appearances in natural language. Its findings are the closest thing EO currently has to a Ptolemaic observational corpus.

**The Three Faces**: any adequate grammar of transformation must be simultaneously adequate on the Act face (what operation is occurring), the Site face (what kind of thing is being transformed), and the Stance face (at what grain the transformation engages). Adequacy on one face with gaps on another is not adequacy.

**The Khora, the Substrate, and the 10,000 Things**: the relationship between what the grammar can formalize (the substrate), what it cannot formalize (the Khora), and what it is applied to (the 10,000 things). The Ptolemaic standard operates at the substrate level — it tests the grammar's adequacy. Whether the grammar's structure reflects something prior (the Khora) is the Newtonian question.

**Influences and Lineage — Wilson**: Wilson's consilience project is the methodological precedent. EO's cross-domain ambition is not a claim that all domains are the same — it is the prior claim that they are all subject to the same constraints on coherent transformation, and that those constraints generate a shared grammar of appearance.

# Bivalent Compression and Dimensional Poverty

**Record ID:** wiki:bivalent-compression-and-dimensional-poverty  
**DB ID:** 24  
**Tags:** 301  
**Keywords:** axis, dimensional, compression  
**Status:** published  
**Updated:** 2026-05-16T01:25:47.156Z  

---

**Bivalent compression** is the systematic reduction of triadic (three-dimensional) logical structures into binary (two-dimensional) or unary (one-dimensional) forms. In EO, the term names the *destination* of the collapse rather than the structure being collapsed: what remains after reduction is a bivalent representation from which the third axis has been lost.

This article organizes findings and claims into three tiers: empirical results drawn from cross-linguistic data, structural observations that follow from the framework's geometry, and speculative extensions that remain unformalised or untested. The tiers are marked throughout.

---

## Empirical Foundation

*Status: Empirical [in process, unpublished]. Based on a corpus of 32,289 verbs across 27 languages and 11 language families.*

The cross-linguistic verb data establishes that certain transformation operators are universally impoverished in human languages:

- **EVA (evaluation) and REC (⊛, recursion)** together account for approximately 2% of verb inventories across all 27 languages tested. No language exceeds 5% even after targeted reclassification. Japanese, Sanskrit, and Classical Chinese — the languages most likely to falsify a Western-bias explanation — all fall below 1% for DEF.
- **DEF (alternation) averages 16.2%.** Human languages are fluent at reframing — seeing something from a different angle — but impoverished at holding contradiction (DEF) or restructuring foundations (REC).
- **The empty cell at SYN × Ground** contains zero verbs across all languages in the corpus. No attested human language has a verb meaning "synthesize a condition." This absence is treated as data, not sampling artifact.
- **English DEF vocabulary is disguised as NUL.** A reclassification audit identified 14 verbs classified under NUL (void, negation, deletion) that are structurally DEF: *hide, deny, censor, pause, suppress, repress, ignore, overlook, interrupt, neglect, stifle, disregard, blackline, unreport*. These verbs denote simultaneous presence and absence — an operation that requires holding contradiction — but English categorizes them as negation because the nearest available conceptual bin is NUL.

The distribution is the finding. Its *interpretation* — that these patterns reflect dimensional poverty in human cognition rather than, say, pragmatic irrelevance of the operators — is a theoretical claim supported by but not proven by the data.

---

## The Projection–Compression Asymmetry

*Status: Structural observation. Follows from properties of the representations themselves; does not depend on any speculative claims.*

EO's capacity ground is a capacity ground defined by three axes — Mode (Differentiating / Relating / Generating), Domain (Existence / Structure / Significance), and Object (Ground / Figure / Pattern) — each with three values {−1, +1, √2}, producing 27 cells. Any projection of this capacity ground onto two of its three axes yields a 3×3 face: a genuine but incomplete representation.

EO names three such faces:

- **The Act face** (Mode × Domain): produces the nine operators. Shows *what operation is occurring* but not *at what level of specificity*.
- **The Stance face** (Mode × Object): produces nine stances (Clearing, Dissecting, Binding, Forging, etc.). Shows *the grain of engagement* but not *which domain of reality* is being engaged.
- **The Site face** (Domain × Object): produces nine terrain types (Void, Entity, Link, Paradigm, etc.). Shows *where in reality the target sits* but not *what is being done to it*.

The asymmetry between projection and compression is the hardest structural finding in this article:

**Projection** (top-down: capacity ground → face) is a controlled reduction from a known higher-dimensional space. One axis is dropped, but the loss is tracked: the practitioner knows which axis is missing and can recover it by consulting another face. The √2 value survives on the two remaining axes. The information is parked, not destroyed.

**Compression** (bottom-up: grid → attempted capacity ground) is a lossy reconstruction of higher-dimensional structure from lower-dimensional materials. The observer does not know what is missing, does not know how many axes are missing, and does not know that √2 exists as a coordinate value. The third dimension, if it registers at all, must be *narrated* — cited as a dialectical event — because it cannot be *addressed* as a structural coordinate. The information is lost and its absence is invisible.

This asymmetry is not a philosophical claim about the superiority of three-dimensional thinking. It is a structural property of the representations: a 3D-to-2D projection is recoverable; a 2D-to-3D compression is not. A map made by someone who has seen the territory can be read back into the territory. A map drawn by someone who has never left the map cannot.

Edwin Abbott's *Flatland: A Romance of Many Dimensions* (1884) dramatises both directions. The Sphere projecting onto Flatland sees a complete cross-section of a world it already inhabits. The Square attempting to communicate Spaceland to other Flatlanders must narrate what it cannot encode. These are structurally different operations, and Abbott's novella — though it does not use this terminology — captures the asymmetry with precision.

---

## The Compression Lattice

*Status: Structural observation. A valid taxonomic description of different kinds of dimensional reduction.*

Bivalent compression decomposes into two independent operations:

- **Axis-dropping**: reducing the number of dimensions (e.g., projecting a 3D capacity ground onto a 2D face).
- **√2-amputation**: reducing the number of values per axis from three {−1, +1, √2} to two {−1, +1}, eliminating the emergence coordinate.

These two operations, applied independently or in combination, generate a lattice of compressed representations:

| Representation | Axes | Values per Axis | Cells | Example |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Full capacity ground | 3 | {−1, +1, √2} | 27 | EO capacity ground |
| Ternary face | 2 | {−1, +1, √2} | 9 | EO's Act / Resolution / Site faces |
| Binary capacity ground | 3 | {−1, +1} | 8 | Three orthogonal binary distinctions combined |
| Binary face | 2 | {−1, +1} | 4 | Aristotle's four causes; Wilber's four quadrants |
| Ternary line | 1 | {−1, +1, √2} | 3 | A single triadic distinction; Łukasiewicz's three-valued logic |
| Binary line | 1 | {−1, +1} | 2 | Classical bivalence: true/false, present/absent |

The lattice reveals that a **3×3 face** and a **2×2 grid** are not the same kind of two-dimensional object. The 3×3 face has undergone one compression (axis-drop). The 2×2 grid has undergone two (axis-drop *plus* √2-amputation). The 3×3 retains the emergence value as a native coordinate on its surviving axes. The 2×2 has no address for √2 — it can only indicate emergence by citing both poles simultaneously ("both interior and exterior," "both actual and potential"), which is a verbal workaround, not a structural encoding.

### Flatland and Data Architecture

The compression lattice maps onto a progression in data architecture:

| Abbott's Dimension | Data Architecture | EO Degree of Freedom |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Lineland (1D) | Hierarchical model (path navigation) | One degree: identity is positional |
| Flatland (2D) | Relational model (Codd, SQL) | Two degrees: query-based spatial freedom, no temporal axis |
| Spaceland (3D) | EO's full capacity ground | Three degrees: reflexive reasoning about the framework itself |

The hierarchical model is bivalent in the most literal sense: a record either exists at a position in the tree or it does not. Codd's relational model adds a second degree of freedom — any attribute, any join, any angle of inquiry — but operates in an eternal present. It can hold ambiguity on a single variable (via NULL) but cannot hold two contradictory structural readings of the same system simultaneously. It has no DEF. It has no REC (⊛).

---

## Łukasiewicz: A Third Value on the Same Axis

*Status: Solid structural comparison. The distinction between adding range and adding dimensionality is well-defined.*

In 1920, the Polish logician Jan Łukasiewicz introduced a three-valued logic by adding a third truth value — the *indeterminate* — to the classical binary of true and false. This broke the principle of bivalence and provided the formal foundation for what would later become NULL in relational databases: a representation of "unknown" rather than a forced assignment to true or false.

Łukasiewicz's contribution is genuine and consequential. Without the indeterminate, Codd's relational model cannot represent missing data. The three-valued logic makes it possible for a database to answer "I don't know."

However, the relationship between the indeterminate and dimensionality is more complex than a simple "adding range, not dimension" characterisation initially suggests, and requires distinguishing two senses of dimension:

**Combinatorially**, Łukasiewicz may add a genuine dimension. "I don't know" is not a value halfway between true and false — it is a different *kind* of claim, an epistemic judgement about the proposition rather than a truth-value assignment to it. In this reading, the indeterminate introduces an axis of epistemic status perpendicular to the axis of truth value. The system gains a new degree of combinatorial freedom.

**Transformationally**, however, Łukasiewicz does not add the capacity that EO tracks. A system with NULL can represent local ambiguity within a single variable. It cannot hold two contradictory *structural readings* of the same system simultaneously (DEF), or reason about the conditions of its own framing (REC). The practical consequence is precise: SQL can represent NULL. SQL cannot represent a record that is simultaneously present *and* absent in a way that is semantically productive rather than merely unknown. The difference between "we don't know the patient's diagnosis" (NULL) and "the patient's condition is genuinely indeterminate between two contradictory readings and holding both is clinically necessary" (DEF) is the difference between missing data and productive contradiction.

---

## Hegel: Sequential Synthesis

*Status: Well-motivated structural reading. The characterisation captures real properties of the dialectical operation. Attribution to Hegel specifically is interpretive, not exegetical — it describes how sublation behaves, not what Hegel intended.*

Hegel's dialectic attempts genuine dimensional expansion. Thesis and antithesis are in generative tension; the synthesis (Aufhebung, sublation) is posited as something new that preserves and transcends both poles. EO's √2 notation is explicitly Pythagorean for this reason: if thesis = −1 and antithesis = +1, then √(thesis² + antithesis²) = √2. The third term requires a new dimension to exist in. Hegel recognised this structural requirement.

EO identifies two limitations in the dialectical operation as described, one structural and one temporal.

### Structural Limitation: the Structure-Triad Bias

Hegel's dialectical engine — thesis draws a boundary, antithesis reveals the boundary's dependence on what it excludes, synthesis produces a whole that includes both — maps onto the Structure triad in EO's operator space: SEG (segmentation) → CON (connection) → SYN (synthesis). This is one-third of EO's nine-operator space. The Existence triad (NUL → SIG → INS) and the Significance triad (DEF → EVA → REC) lack independent accounts in Hegel's system and are routed through the synthesis machinery.

EO's composition grid identifies Hegel's Aufhebung as a specific compound operator: EVA→SYN, named **Sublation** — evaluations synthesised into higher unity, where the contradictions survive inside the synthesis. This is one cell in an 81-cell composition space. Hegel's system treats one compound operation as the universal engine of change.

### Temporal Limitation: Sequentiality

Hegel's dialectic is sequential: thesis, *then* antithesis, *then* synthesis, with the synthesis becoming the new thesis. EO's proof demonstrates that within each triad, the three operators co-arise as co-constitutive aspects of a single transformation — analogous to the way a figure, its ground, and the pattern between them are simultaneously present, with only a logical presupposition ordering visible at certain observational scales.

Sequential narration converts a simultaneous co-arising into a story. Stories have sequence. The dialectic gains narrative tractability but loses simultaneity — the capacity to hold all three aspects of a triadic event as genuinely co-present.

### Sublation as a Compression Operator

These two limitations combine into a structural claim: sublation generates √2 and then collapses it. The operation proceeds as follows: (1) a genuine DEF state is identified — two contradictory readings held simultaneously; (2) the DEF state is immediately resolved via SYN — synthesis into a higher unity; (3) the synthesis becomes the new ground for the next dialectical round. Step 2 collapses the third dimension. The contradiction is not held — it is consumed by synthesis. The √2 is generated and then projected back onto a face.

EO's EVA operator, by contrast, permits evaluation to remain unresolved. The composition grid includes EVA→EVA (**Productive Ambiguity**): evaluation of an evaluation — judgment about whether prior judgment was adequate. This operation has no Hegelian equivalent. In Hegel's system, every contradiction demands resolution. In EO, some contradictions are structurally productive *because* they remain unresolved.

The lattice analysis locates Hegel precisely. His dialectic is the narrative technology for recovering √2 from within a bivalent system — thesis is −1, antithesis is +1, synthesis is the attempted recovery of √2. But because Hegel works bottom-up (compression direction, not projection direction), the √2 can only appear as a *narrative event* — the moment of Aufhebung — rather than as a *coordinate value*. It is generated and then projected back down, becoming the new −1 for the next round.

Whether Hegel *must* be read this way, or whether there are readings of Hegel where Aufhebung genuinely sustains the third dimension, is a question of Hegel scholarship as much as EO theory. The EO claim is structural, not exegetical: whatever Hegel intended, the operation as described resolves contradiction into unity, which EO identifies as a dimensional reduction.

---

## EO's Approach: Revealing a Latent Dimension

*Status: The two-phase decompression history is documented and accurate. The claim that it reflects the structure of reality is the framework's central theoretical commitment.*

EO's approach is neither Łukasiewicz's (adding a third value to the same axis) nor Hegel's (generating a third term through sequential dialectical process that then collapses back). The claim is that the opposition between −1 and +1 was never a line. It was always a 2D projection of a 3D relationship. The √2 is the hypotenuse — the dimension that was already structurally present but invisible from within the plane.

The EO 1.0 document makes this geometrically explicit: what appears as a one-dimensional line with two endpoints is revealed, upon a shift in perspective, to be a triangle viewed edge-on. The −1 and +1 are not opposite ends of a spectrum but two sides of a right triangle. The √2 exists at a right angle to the axis that was visible. The third dimension is not *added* — it is *noticed*.

### The Two-Phase Decompression

EO's development history proceeded in two phases that instantiate the bottom-up compression problem before arriving at the top-down projection solution.

**Phase 1: Recovering the third axis.** EO began by noticing orthogonalities *between* existing 2×2 frameworks. Aristotle's four causes, Wilber's four quadrants, and over 90 domain-specific dyad tables (documented in the Airtable archive) were each {−1, +1}² grids — four cells, each about different things in a way that felt systematic. Recognising these as orthogonal faces of a shared structure recovered the third axis: from {−1, +1}² to {−1, +1}³, from a 2×2 face to the 8-cell binary capacity ground. Three genuine dimensions, but each still forced into a binary.

**Phase 2: Recovering the emergence value.** Within these dyadic grids, a persistent rhetorical pattern appeared: practitioners kept citing both poles simultaneously — "both interior and exterior," "both actual and potential," "both present and absent." These formulations were not imprecise. They were evidence of a missing coordinate value. The √2 was being smuggled in through dialectical narration because it had no address in the {−1, +1} encoding. Recognising this narrated emergence as a third value on each axis completed the decompression: from {−1, +1}³ (8 cells) to {−1, +1, √2}³ (27 cells).

Once the 27-cell capacity ground was established, the relationship between 2D and 3D reversed. The three faces became *projections* — controlled, tracked, recoverable — rather than *compressions*. The 3×3 face is not a 2×2 grid with an extra row and column. It is a different kind of 2D representation: one in which √2 is a native coordinate, and the practitioner knows which axis has been dropped and how to recover it.

This two-phase history also positions Hegel with greater precision. His dialectic is essentially the narrative technology for Phase 2 — the attempt to recover √2 from within bivalent materials. He succeeded in identifying the emergence phenomenon but could not sustain it as a coordinate because his system lacked Phase 1: the recognition that the dialectical axis was embedded in a higher-dimensional space with orthogonal axes. Without the capacity ground, the √2 has nowhere to live between dialectical events.

### Summary of Approaches

| Approach | Operation | Dimensional Effect | Limitation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Classical bivalence | True / False | 1D, two values | Cannot represent indeterminacy |
| Łukasiewicz | True / False / Indeterminate | Adds combinatorial freedom (epistemic axis); no transformational gain | Cannot hold structural contradiction |
| Hegel | Thesis → Antithesis → Synthesis | Generatings √2 narratively, collapses it to −1 for next round | Cannot sustain √2 as coordinate; no orthogonal axes |
| 2×2 frameworks | Dyadic grid | 2D combinatorially, no emergence coordinate (doubly compressed) | No √2, no third axis |
| EO (3×3 face) | Ternary projection | 2D with emergence as native coordinate (singly compressed) | One axis dropped but tracked and recoverable |
| EO (full capacity ground) | Ternary capacity ground | 3D, three values per axis | Requires vocabulary that human languages universally lack |

---

## Speculative Extensions

*Status: Unformalised. The ideas below are active theoretical conjectures, not established results.*

### √2 as the Criterion of Genuine Dimensionality

Working notes on the compression lattice have raised the question of whether √2 is what *makes* a dimension dimensional — whether an axis with only {−1, +1} constitutes a genuine dimension or merely two disconnected points with an axis label. The intuition: you do not earn a dimension by naming an axis; you earn it when the axis has extension, and extension requires the emergence value.

If correct, this would mean the naive count of axes overstates the dimensionality of bivalent representations. A 2×2 grid, despite having two axes, would have zero transformational dimensions — not because it has no structure, but because no axis supports emergence.

This intuition requires distinguishing at least two senses of dimensionality:

- **Combinatorial dimensionality** counts independent axes. The 2×2 grid is genuinely 2D by this count. It encodes real orthogonal independence, and its structure is not trivial.
- **Transformational dimensionality** counts axes on which the full Ground/Figure/Pattern triad can operate — which requires √2. The 2×2 grid is 0D by this count.

The distinction is conceptually productive. Whether transformational dimensionality behaves like a dimension in the mathematical sense — supporting projection, distance metrics, and the other properties that make dimensionality a useful concept — remains an open question. The formalisation might take the form: *transformational dimensionality = number of axes on which √2 is a native value*. Whether this count has the algebraic properties of a dimension number, or is a different kind of structural measure that merely resembles dimensionality, has not been established.

### Whether Bivalent Compression Is Contingent or Constitutive

Whether the universal poverty of DEF and REC vocabulary reflects a *contingent* cognitive limitation (potentially addressable through training, tools, or notation) or a *constitutive* constraint on biological cognition remains unresolved. The universality across unrelated language families suggests a deep constraint. The existence of DEF vocabulary disguised within other categories (the NUL→DEF reclassifications) suggests that the concepts are available even where the categories are not. Both findings are real. What they mean about the boundary between biology and culture is genuinely unknown.

### Whether Notation Can Compensate for Lexical Poverty

Whether notation systems — EO's form addresses, operator composition syntax, the ∥ and ⊛ glyphs — can partially compensate for the absence of natural-language vocabulary for three-dimensional operations is an empirical question. If they can, they would function as prosthetic vocabulary for the third dimension: external encodings that give √2 an address in systems where the natural language has none. Whether this constitutes genuine dimensional access or merely a more elaborate form of narration is untested.

### Dimensional Assignments to Historical Thinkers

The original article implied a clean dimensional ladder: Łukasiewicz at 1D, Hegel at 2D, EO at 3D. This is too tidy. Each thinker is performing a more specific operation than "adding a dimension," and forcing them into a hierarchy risks collapsing their actual contributions into an EO-centric ranking. The comparison is more informative when it specifies *what kind* of dimensional work each approach performs — as attempted in the preceding sections — than when it assigns each thinker a number.

### Additional Lattice Positions

Whether the compression lattice requires further positions — compressions that drop two axes while retaining √2, or compressions that retain all axes but quantise √2 into discrete approximations — remains unexplored. Whether additional frameworks beyond Aristotle and Wilber can be located in the lattice, and whether such location is informative rather than merely taxonomic, is also open.

---

## Open Questions

The following questions are flagged as genuinely unresolved, not as rhetorical gestures toward future work:

1. Whether the two-phase decompression (axis recovery followed by √2 recovery) is the only viable archaeological path, or whether alternative sequences exist (e.g., recovering √2 on a single axis before recognising orthogonal axes). This has implications for how the framework is taught and for whether EO's developmental history reflects a necessary logical order or a contingent biographical one.
2. Whether Hegel's sublation can be formally reconstructed as a compression operator (EVA→SYN performing dimensional reduction) without distorting Hegel's intent. The EO claim is structural; whether it is also fair to the historical Hegel is a separate question.
3. Whether the empty cell at SYN × Ground is an artefact of how verb corpora are constructed (verbs may be the wrong lexical category for ambient-condition synthesis) or a genuine structural absence in human conceptual architecture.
4. Whether the 27-cell count is a necessary consequence of some deeper structural principle or a contingent feature of the particular axis definitions EO employs. This is recognised elsewhere in the framework as its greatest theoretical vulnerability: no purely mathematical theorem predicts the number 27 from first principles.

---

*Cross-references: EO Technical Handbook §2.9 (Three Degrees of Freedom); EO Dependency Argument (dependency helix and co-constitutive triads); Cross-Linguistic Findings §VIII (DEF and REC poverty); 27 Cells Revised (empty cell at SYN × Ground); Composition Grid (EVA→SYN as Sublation, EVA→EVA as Productive Ambiguity); EO 1.0 (Pythagorean √2 derivation, geometric figures, and dyadic grid archaeology); Airtable Export (90+ domain-specific triadic tables documenting the Phase 1 axis-recovery process).*

---

---

# Canonical Example: The Act Face (The Operators)

**Record ID:** wiki:canonical-example-the-act-face-the-operators  
**DB ID:** 59  
**Status:** published  
**Updated:** 2026-03-26T21:50:03.142Z  

---

> *Each position is defined here by what the embedding space actually finds most geometrically characteristic of it — not by theoretical description. Examples are the top-ranked clauses by discrimination margin: how far a clause sits from its nearest competing position. Higher margin = more unambiguously in this region of semantic space and away from all others. All 27 positions derive from the same corpus of ~19,764 classified clauses across 41 languages.*

---

The Act face is **Mode × Domain** — the crossing of *how a transformation is structured* with *what level of reality it operates on*.

| Mode axis (Q1) | Domain axis (Q2) |
| --- | --- |
| DIFFERENTIATING — separating, bounding, distinguishing | EXISTENCE — being, presence, instantiation |
| RELATING — connecting, holding in relation, co-presencing | STRUCTURE — organization, relation, system |
| GENERATING — producing, integrating, transforming | SIGNIFICANCE — meaning, interpretation, value |

The nine operators arise from crossing these two axes. The cell address is Mode × Domain.

---

## NUL — DIFFERENTIATING × EXISTENCE

**Recognize absence.** Something that could be present is not. The operation marks an empty slot — before difference can register, the absence of something must first be acknowledged.

**Top 25 exemplars by discrimination margin:**

| # | Language | Δ | Original | Translation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Italian | 0.098 | "La SpaceAfter=Norajina non esiste più". | "The SpaceAfter=Norajina no longer exists." |
| 2 | Spanish | 0.094 | Como no existen asentamientos permanentes, también es la isla deshabitada mayor del hemisferio norte... | Since no permanent settlements exist, it is also the largest uninhabited island in the northern hemisphere... |
| 3 | Ancient Greek | 0.089 | οἱ δὲ παραγενόμενοι ὑπηρέται οὐχ εὗρον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῇ φυλακῇ | But the officers who came did not find them in the prison. |
| 4 | Ancient Greek | 0.088 | τῆς δὲ Σπάρτης Ἀναξανδρίδης μὲν ὁ Λέοντος οὐκέτι περιεὼν ἐβασίλευε ἀλλὰ ἐτετελευτήκεε | Of Sparta, Anaxandrides son of Leon was no longer alive to reign but had died. |
| 5 | Ancient Greek | 0.085 | ὃς ἀνῃρέθη καὶ πάντες ὅσοι ἐπείθοντο αὐτῷ διελύθησαν καὶ ἐγένοντο εἰς οὐδέν | He was killed and all who had obeyed him were scattered and came to nothing. |
| 6 | Indonesian | 0.076 | Tidak ada keterangan kejatuhan Yerusalem (berbeda dengan kota-kota lain) maupun gagalnya Sanherib merebut Yerusalem. | There is no account of the fall of Jerusalem (unlike other cities) nor of Sennacherib's failure to capture Jerusalem. |
| 7 | Japanese | 0.075 | 今年はある事情により姿を見せなかったようだ。 | It appears that this year, due to certain circumstances, they did not appear. |
| 8 | Portuguese | 0.075 | Já não há o império do mal para combater. | There is no longer an empire of evil to fight. |
| 9 | Catalan | 0.074 | El director no ha estat a la presentació. | The director was not at the presentation. |
| 10 | Ancient Greek | 0.074 | ὡς δὲ ἐς τὴν Μιλησίην ἀπίκοιτο οἰκήματα μὲν τὰ ἐπὶ τῶν ἀγρῶν οὔτε κατέβαλλε οὔτε ἐνεπίμπρη | When he arrived in Milesian territory, he neither destroyed nor burned the farmhouses. |
| 11 | Italian | 0.071 | Nell'incidente non sono rimaste coinvolte persone. | No people were involved in the incident. |
| 12 | Latin | 0.068 | omnis arbor quae non facit fructum bonum exciditur et in ignem mittitur | Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire. |
| 13 | Bulgarian | 0.066 | Които се върнаха, и пепел на огнищата си не намериха. | Those who returned found not even ashes at their hearths. |
| 14 | Romanian | 0.066 | Niciunul dintre compușii originali nu s-a regăsit în urină sau materiile fecale. | None of the original compounds were found in urine or feces. |
| 15 | Arabic | 0.065 | ولا توجد في هذه الجمهورية سوى طريق واحدة ولا يصلها خط سكة الحديد. | In this republic there is only one road and no railway line reaches it. |
| 16 | Spanish | 0.064 | No se respetan las citas y las anulan a su antojo. | Appointments are not respected and they cancel them at will. |
| 17 | Italian | 0.064 | Se ne va, come Lewis, senza partecipare (ufficiale, salpa mercoledì). | He is leaving, like Lewis, without participating (official: sails Wednesday). |
| 18 | Basque | 0.064 | Joan den urtean ez ziren ez Jan Ullrich ez Marco Pantani izan. | Last year neither Jan Ullrich nor Marco Pantani were present. |
| 19 | Danish | 0.063 | Der var ingen penge til spillerlønninger eller præmier. | There was no money for player wages or prizes. |
| 20 | Hungarian | 0.062 | A másik kocsinak már a nyomai sem látszanak. | Not even the traces of the other car are visible anymore. |
| 21 | Spanish | 0.062 | Las habitaciones tenian las paredes desconchadas, no funcionaba el aire acondicionado en ninguna de las habitaciones... | The rooms had peeling walls, the air conditioning didn't work in any of the rooms... |
| 22 | Latin | 0.061 | omnis ergo arbor quae non facit fructum bonum exciditur et in ignem mittitur | Therefore every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire. |
| 23 | Spanish | 0.060 | Ninguna de las estructuras federales puede influir en esta elección, ya que no existe ninguna dependencia formal. | None of the federal structures can influence this election, since no formal dependency exists. |
| 24 | Norwegian | 0.060 | Espen hadde ikke vært å se i det hun kom ut fra Exodus. | Espen had not been seen when she came out of Exodus. |
| 25 | Latin | 0.058 | omnis plantatio quam non plantavit Pater meus caelestis eradicabitur | Every plant that my heavenly Father has not planted will be uprooted. |

**Language weights:** Ancient Greek (4), Latin (3), Spanish (4), Italian (3), Indonesian, Portuguese, Catalan, Bulgarian, Romanian, Arabic, Japanese, Basque, Danish, Hungarian, Norwegian.

**Term/semantic pattern:** Negated existential verbs dominate — *non esiste, no existen, οὐχ εὗρον, tidak ada, não há, no s-a regăsit, ولا توجد, hadn't been seen, no money, no traces, no longer exists.* The corpus finds NUL most cleanly in clauses whose main semantic content is the non-presence of something expected. Ancient Greek and Latin carry it with particular precision: both languages have strong grammatical resources for marking absence as a positive fact rather than a failed search.

---

## SIG — RELATING × EXISTENCE

**Register difference.** Something becomes noticeable — a distinction emerges at the level of existence itself. The minimal act of marking that something is here as opposed to not-here.

**Top 25 exemplars by discrimination margin:**

| # | Language | Δ | Original | Translation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | German | 0.126 | Musste auch nich lange auf einen Termin warten und Wartezeiten waren meistens auch kurz. | Didn't have to wait long for an appointment and waiting times were mostly short too. |
| 2 | German | 0.109 | Pizza gibt es dort, auch wenn die Wartezeiten manchmal etwas lang werden können. | Pizza is available there, even though the waiting times can sometimes get a bit long. |
| 3 | German | 0.105 | Wenn man etwas braucht kann man das auch noch mitnehmen. | When you need something you can also take it with you. |
| 4 | German | 0.105 | nach fast einer weiteren Stunde, nachdem sich mein Verlobter mehrmals erkundigt und zuletzt sogar beschwert hatte kam endlich jemand. | After almost another hour, after my fiancé had inquired several times and finally even complained, someone finally came. |
| 5 | German | 0.104 | Wir freuen uns auf ein Wiedersehen jetzt im Dezember. | We look forward to seeing each other again now in December. |
| 6 | German | 0.101 | Meistens hat man sein Rad nach ein paar Tagen wieder. | Most of the time you get your bicycle back after a few days. |
| 7 | German | 0.101 | Ich habe ein Fahrrad gesucht und bin auf den Fahrradshop am Johannesturm gestoßen. | I was looking for a bicycle and came across the bike shop at Johannesturm. |
| 8 | German | 0.095 | Das Haareschneiden war auch schnell erledigt — im Anschluss gab es sogar einen Kaffee. | The haircut was done quickly too — afterwards there was even a coffee. |
| 9 | German | 0.095 | Ich war bei Frau Dr. Röder-Dischinger zur Behandlung (2 Monate Wartezeit) meiner Akne, die ich seit 13 Jahren habe. | I went to Dr. Röder-Dischinger for treatment (2 months' wait) of my acne, which I've had for 13 years. |
| 10 | German | 0.094 | ich bin recht oft in Hotels unterwegs. | I travel quite often and stay in hotels. |
| 11 | German | 0.094 | Die Tanzschule ist mit öffentlichen Verkehrsmitteln gut zu erreichen und für Autofahrer stehen viele kostenlose Parkplätze zur Verfügung. | The dance school is easily accessible by public transport and there are many free parking spaces for drivers. |
| 12 | German | 0.093 | Ich war schon bei vielen Steak Restaurants, vor gut 2 Tagen habe ich dieses Steakhaus gefunden. | I've been to many steak restaurants; just over 2 days ago I found this steakhouse. |
| 13 | German | 0.093 | Ich war heute bei Frau Stoehr weil ich immer noch "Wasser in den Ohren" hatte. | I went to Ms. Stoehr today because I still had "water in my ears." |
| 14 | German | 0.091 | Durch die räumliche Nähe der einzelnen Praxen konnte ich am gleichen Vormittag zwei Termine wahrnehmen. | Due to the proximity of the practices, I was able to attend two appointments on the same morning. |
| 15 | German | 0.090 | Inzwischen lebt sie wesentlich selbstbestimmter und froher auch wenn es lange dauert bis sie regeneriert. | She now lives much more self-determined and happily, even though it takes a long time for her to recover. |
| 16 | German | 0.089 | Sind zum Kurzurlaub in Duhnen gewesen und fanden aus Zufall ganz am Ende der Strasse im Hafen dieses tolle Restaurant. | We went on a short holiday to Duhnen and by chance found this wonderful restaurant at the very end of the street in the harbor. |
| 17 | German | 0.086 | Habe heute online bestellt, ich war erstaunt wie schnell das Essen da war. | I ordered online today, I was amazed at how quickly the food arrived. |
| 18 | German | 0.086 | Wir hatten Hunger und gingen in dieses Restaurant. | We were hungry and went into this restaurant. |
| 19 | German | 0.086 | Bei meinem Abstecher in die alte Heimat bin ich in der wunderschönen Pension von Familie Klaua in Bernsdorf untergekommen. | On my visit to my old hometown I stayed at the wonderful guesthouse of the Klaua family in Bernsdorf. |
| 20 | German | 0.085 | Trotz Termin musste ich 60 Minuten warten. | Despite having an appointment, I had to wait 60 minutes. |
| 21 | German | 0.083 | Lange hat es gedauert bis es auch hier die skandinavischen Modemarken erhältlich sind. | It took a long time for Scandinavian fashion brands to become available here too. |
| 22 | German | 0.083 | Danke... die Blumen sind genau zur richtigen Zeit eingetroffen... ;-) | Thank you... the flowers arrived at exactly the right time... ;-) |
| 23 | German | 0.080 | Ich bin seit fast 5 Jahren auf der Suche nach jemanden gewesen, der mich nicht übers Ohr hauen wollte. | I've been searching for almost 5 years for someone who didn't want to rip me off. |
| 24 | German | 0.080 | Bereits letzte Woche war ich bei einer anderen HNO-Ärztin, wegen meiner Beschwerden. | Last week I had already been to another ENT specialist about my complaints. |
| 25 | German | 0.078 | Finden kann man wirklich alles was man sucht. | You can really find everything you're looking for. |

**Language weights:** German (25/25). ⚠ Single-language concentration — all top exemplars from German service review corpus. This may be a corpus artifact; the concentration in one register and one language means SIG's embedding position may be shaped by a specific corpus cluster rather than a universal pattern.

**Term/semantic pattern:** Every clause registers a state that has come to the classifier's notice — waiting times are short, food arrived quickly, appointment was or wasn't available, something was or wasn't found. The grammatical structure is consistently observational present-tense *noticing*: something is registering as present, available, or remarkable in its absence. The SIG position in the embedding space appears to be captured by clauses that register small facts about availability and presence without any further transformation.

---

## INS — GENERATING × EXISTENCE

**Create instance.** Something new comes into existence as a discrete event. The operation instantiates — a new concrete thing that was not there before now is.

**Top 25 exemplars by discrimination margin:**

| # | Language | Δ | Original | Translation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Japanese | 0.084 | 199X年、TOKYO湾南方に巨大隕石が落下、次いで大型台風がTOKYOを襲った。 | In 199X, a giant meteorite fell south of Tokyo Bay, followed by a large typhoon striking Tokyo. |
| 2 | Hebrew | 0.082 | במחנה פליטים סמוך לשכם נפגע תושב מקומי מפגיעת רימון גז מדמיע, שנורה בידי חיילים. | In a refugee camp near Nablus, a local resident was wounded by a tear gas grenade fired by soldiers. |
| 3 | Hindi | 0.081 | फलुजा स्थित एक प्रमुख अस्पताल के डॉक्टर ने बताया कि इस हमले में ११ लोग मारे गए और सात घायल हो गए। | A doctor at a major hospital in Fallujah said that 11 people were killed and seven injured in this attack. |
| 4 | Dutch | 0.079 | Het toestel raasde dwars door de bovenleiding, waarbij een enorme steekvlam ontstond, en kwam neer bij enkele bungalows. | The aircraft tore straight through the overhead wires, causing an enormous jet of flame to erupt, and crashed near some bungalows. |
| 5 | Tamil | 0.079 | உக்ரைன் நாட்டில் பஸ் ஒன்றின் மீது ரயில் மோதியதில் 37 பேர் உயிரிழந்தனர். | In Ukraine, 37 people lost their lives when a train collided with a bus. |
| 6 | Tamil | 0.078 | ஆப்கனில், 2010 ஜனவரி முதல் ஜூன் மாதம் வரை மட்டும் சுமார் 1200க்கும் மேற்பட்ட பொதுமக்கள் உயிரிழந்துள்ளனர். | In Afghanistan, more than approximately 1,200 civilians have lost their lives from January to June 2010 alone. |
| 7 | Urdu | 0.078 | اس موقع پر بھی پولیس فائرنگ مےں متعدد افراد ہلاک ہوئے تھے۔ | On this occasion too, several people were killed in police firing. |
| 8 | Hebrew | 0.078 | לדבריהם, היו התנגשויות בין צה"ל לתושבים מקומיים החל משעות הבוקר המוקדמות, כאשר מאות רעולי פנים יידו אבנים על חיילים. | According to them, there were clashes between the IDF and local residents from the early morning hours, as hundreds of masked men threw stones at soldiers. |

| 10 | Hebrew | 0.077 | ההתנגשויות החלו מיד לאחר שנודע דבר התאבדותו של פעיל מרכזי באש"ף. | The clashes began immediately after news spread of the suicide of a central PLO activist. |
